1: The narrative transition: after Jeremiah finishes speaking "all the words of the LORD their God (kol dibrê YHWH ʾĕlōhêhem) — every word the LORD their God had sent him to tell them" — the community's response is rejection. The redundancy ("all the words... every word") emphasizes the completeness of the message given; there is no remaining word that could have changed their minds. Rejection of comprehensive prophecy is total rejection.
2: wayyōʾmer ʿAzaryāhû ben-Hôšaʿyāhû... lōʾ šāqer ʾattâ mĕdabbēr — "Azariah son of Hoshaiah... said to Jeremiah: You are speaking falsely!" Šeqer (falsehood) — the same word Jeremiah used against Hananiah and the false prophets. Now the accusation of false prophecy is turned against the authentic prophet. The MKT renders the blunt accusation: "You are speaking falsely!" The exclamation is appropriate to the direct verbal attack.
3: kî Bārûk ben-Nĕriyyāhû massît ʾōtĕkā bānû — "But Baruch son of Neriah is inciting you against us, to deliver us into the hands of the Chaldeans." The scapegoating of Baruch: the accusation that Jeremiah's secretary is the real source of the anti-Egypt advice. Massît (inciting, stirring up) is the vocabulary used of the adversary who incites disobedience (cf. Deuteronomy 13:6). Making Baruch a Chaldean agent redirects blame from YHWH's word onto a human conspirator.
4: wayyišmaʿ Yôḥānān ben-Qārēaḥ wĕkol-śārê haḥăyālîm wĕkol-hāʿām bĕqôl YHWH — "So Johanan son of Kareah and all the commanders of the forces and all the people disobeyed the voice of the LORD (lōʾ šāmĕʿû bĕqôl YHWH), refusing to stay in the land." The word šāmaʿ bĕqôl (obey, listen to the voice) — the covenantal hearing-obedience term — is negated: they did not listen. The MKT renders the refusal directly: "disobeyed the voice of the LORD."
5: The irony of Jeremiah 43:5: Johanan and the commanders took "the entire remnant of Judah who had returned (šābû) from all the nations where they had been scattered" — those who had returned from exile-like scattering are now led away from the land. The double šûb narrative: people who returned are now being taken away again by those who should have led them home.
6: The list of those taken to Egypt: men, women, children, royal daughters (ʾet-bĕnôt hammelek), and those the Babylonian captain had placed under Gedaliah. The "royal daughters" reappear — the Davidic remnant. Also Jeremiah and Baruch are taken (wĕʾet-Yirmĕyāhû hannābîʾ wĕʾet-Bārûk ben-Nĕriyyāhû). The prophet who warned against Egypt is taken to Egypt — the covenant witness goes with the covenant-breakers.
7: wayyābōʾû ʾerets Miṣrayim kî lōʾ šāmĕʿû bĕqôl YHWH — "They entered the land of Egypt, because they would not obey the voice of the LORD." The motive clause is explicit: the refusal to obey is the narrator's final word on why they came to Tahpanhes. The anti-Exodus is complete: the nation that was brought out of Egypt by YHWH's mighty hand has returned to Egypt by its own disobedience.
8: The divine word comes to Jeremiah even in Tahpanhes (in Egypt) — YHWH's presence and speech are not geographically limited to the land of Israel. The oracle that follows is a sign-act (Jeremiah 43:9-13), one of Jeremiah's most unusual. The oracle in Egypt parallels the oracles against Egypt in the OAN section (Jeremiah 46), completing the frame.
9: qaḥ bĕyādĕkā ʾăbānîm gĕdōlôt ûṭĕmantām bammeleṭ bammalben — "Take some large stones in your hands and bury them in the mortar in the brick pavement at the entrance to Pharaoh's palace in Tahpanhes." The sign-act: stones hidden in the pavement of Pharaoh's palace entrance will become the foundation for Nebuchadnezzar's throne. Jeremiah performs this sign publicly, in front of the Jewish community. The MKT renders "mortar in the brick pavement" following MT's bammeleṭ bammalben.
10: hinnĕnî šōlēaḥ ûlāqaḥtî ʾet-Nĕbûkadrĕʾṣar melek Bābel ʿabdî — "I am going to send for Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon, my servant (ʿabdî)." For the third time (Jeremiah 25:9; Jeremiah 27:6) Nebuchadnezzar is designated YHWH's servant — the most theologically shocking claim in Jeremiah's international theology. The pagan conqueror performs YHWH's will. "He will place his throne above these stones I buried" — the Babylonian king will unknowingly sit on the stones Jeremiah buried. The sign-act creates a covenant connection between the prophet's action and the future historical event.
11: The comprehensive judgment on Egypt: ʾăšer lammāwet lammāwet wĕʾăšer laššĕbî laššĕbî wĕʾăšer laḥereb laḥereb — "those destined for death, to death; those destined for captivity, to captivity; those destined for the sword, to the sword" — the fourfold formula from 15:2 applied now to Egypt. The Jews who fled to Egypt for safety will encounter there the same covenant judgment they fled.
12: wĕhiṣṣattî ʾēš bĕbāttê ʾĕlōhê Miṣrayim — "I will set fire to the temples of the gods of Egypt." The Egyptian gods in their temples will be burned. The image of Nebuchadnezzar picking Egypt clean "as a shepherd picks his garment clean of lice" (kĕhitʿaṭṭôp hārōʿeh ʾet-bigdô) — a colloquial image for thoroughness. The MKT preserves the homely simile as a marker of the oracle's confident tone.
13: wĕšibbar ʾet-maṣṣĕbôt Bêt-Šemeš ʾăšer bĕʾerets Miṣrayim — "He will demolish the sacred pillars of Beth-shemesh (Bêt-Šemeš = 'house of the sun') in the land of Egypt." Beth-shemesh in Egypt is Heliopolis — the great temple of the sun-god Ra. The destruction of Ra's sacred pillars (obelisks) by Babylon is the theological climax: even the greatest sun-worship center of the ancient world will fall before YHWH's instrument. The MKT's "Beth-shemesh" preserves the Hebrew name that means "House of the Sun."
